speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 4. Interview with Mr. Kurt Lewin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you asked several times about life in the ghetto, either in Lwow or in Rawa-Ruska. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Probably to understand this, you have to accept the fact that there was no normalcy of any kind. Nothing was predictable or stable. Danger was looming at every moment, in every corner. There was no protection of any law or any authority. A Jew was free victim and prey to everybody. So it was a life of a hunted animal, either hunted by the Germans, or by the Ukrainian police, or by local populace. This is one aspect of this problem. The second aspect of the problem was a terrible hunger, starvation, because food was not available. Or it was available, and local population was selling food to Jews for gold, or precious things, anything, clothes, depriving these poor people of what was left to them. Third problem, by moving from place to place, they left their possessions behind, because you couldn't carry it. And the space was smaller and smaller. So try to imagine here, people who were constantly squeezed and under pressure. So your questions about celebrations, education, and so forth, don't apply. Everybody--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they did happen. People did get married. People did have children.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got married because, you see, people in those days have a concept of correctness and dignity. And people wouldn't live together unless it was done in a traditional way by having a ceremony. End of story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Women got pregnant and gave birth. And people died. People were sick. The only circumstances-- the circumstances were extraordinary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever punished in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Punished by whom? For what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainians, Germans, by [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. There was some criminal police and Jewish criminal police who dealt with criminals, but only to a certain point. I don't believe that there was anything of that sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the criminals? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews were criminals too.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I meant what kind of criminals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thieves, pickpockets, murderers, anything you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these things take place in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you witness to any of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people-- I know you escaped from the ghetto. Was there much escape from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was practically impossible to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, first of all, I've seen the horror of Brygidki, so I realized the full extent of the brutality, danger, and lawlessness. Secondly, as a child, I was usually more independent and more resourceful than the usual Jewish child, which was fearful and brought up to keep away from Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, you see, gave me a much greater initiative. And thirdly, I was privileged because people in the ghetto said, you are very fortunate because you don't look Jewish. You have an enormous asset.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And I spoke the language. And that gave me maneuverability. Having escaped to Rawa-Ruska, I could eat better because food was more accessible there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lwow to Rawa-Ruska? Very simply, I got myself as my wife's-- my mother's cousin-- this job in [NON-ENGLISH], and went there as a collector of rags. End of story. So you had a legal exit from-- Some-- well, surely. And we arrived in Rawa-Ruska, and we lived there. End of story. And from the documents, you'll find that this was organized in a certain way. And certain categories of people were left alone. After that, the pressure started to build up. And there were [? akcja, ?] what you call. [? Akcja ?] or most essentially an organized collection of certain categories of people who were shipped to a death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in any [? akcjas? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Actually, one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in [? akcja, ?] two in Rawa-Ruska and two in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which came first? Rawa-Ruska?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rawa-Ruska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Rawa-Ruska. Were you in the ghetto there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. It was not a defined ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: It had no walls or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the Nazi in Rawa-Ruska?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rawa-Ruska is about-- not more than 18 kilometers from Belzitz. And the camp-- the death camp in Bel-- it was not a concentration camp. It was just simply, no more and no less, gas chambers and crematoria. What people don't realize, that perhaps a million to a million and a half Jews were processed there. That was not far from Rawa-Ruska, 18 kilometers. The-- Belzitz death camp was erected, I think, in January, February 1942, shortly after Wannsee Conference. And the first group of Jews who were caught and taken to be tested, as the construction was completed, were Jews from Rawa-Ruska, 200 of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We knew that they were killed there. We didn't know the method because the Ukrainians were not allowed to go there either. So they said that they killed Jews. How? There were all kinds of versions, by steam, by electricity, and so forth. But they knew that they were killed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was the [? selectia ?] done?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no [? selectia. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the [? akcja? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: They just simply came to town with trucks, with the [NON ENGLISH], which was the military police. It's described in the book which was published here in the United States, Regiment 101 of the reserve police. And they caught, at random, 200 people, put them on trucks, shipped them, and they disappeared. End of story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- we were talking about your being in a [? selectia ?] or an [? akcja. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that? How was it done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Germans were on the ground catching Jews with the help of the Ukrainian police and with the help of local population.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were collected, usually, next to the railroad station and shipped wherever they were shipped.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once-- in the second [? akcja ?] in Rawa-Ruska, we were hiding in the warehouse where the rags were kept inside-- in the rags. And I went to visit somebody. And a Ukrainian teenager grabbed me and started to drag me to the collection or to assembly area. As I told you, that I was a scrappy youngster, so I beat him up and ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from other [? akcjas ?] or [? selectias, ?] how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lwow, the major [? akcja, ?] which probably was one of the largest, 60,000 people were collected. We were hiding in the cellar in the building where my mother lived for a while after leaving her original home. And this cellar, well, I ran out of there and went back to Rawa-Ruska because of arrangements, which I have described in my book. My mother was sticking out of there because Polish women who were sitting there and selling vegetables pointed out to the Germans and to the Ukrainian police, the Jews are there. They were all taken to assembly point. My mother succeeded to pay off Jewish policemen with some jewelry she had. And she and my brothers were returned to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other experiences in the ghetto before you left both Lwow and Rawa-Ruska?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hundreds of them, but I think-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --They amount to the same. What was your greatest fear there, other than being caught, obviously?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fear was very simply to be caught and be brought to a situation, which I found myself in Brygidki.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept my wits with-- and I was alert. I watched around-- watched what is happening around me and lived like an animal, a hunted animal. Period. End.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the narrowest escape, you had there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are not one. There are very many.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your mind, what was the toughest situation you got out of?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was to be shipped to be shot in Lwow, to Piaski, with other members of the Judenrat. Two--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. I jumped out of a truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, I was coming back from a visit in Rawa-Ruska through side streets, and I run into two SS men who were passing-- these were Waffen-SS-- passing through Rawa-Ruska. It was a railroad center. And they accosted me and asked me what am I doing. Well, I said, I am a member of the [NON-ENGLISH] polizei, which means Jewish police health department. And I'm coming back from the hospital were I transported typhoid sick people. When they heard typhoid, they ran away, but this was a pretty close. I was lucky they didn't take a pistol-- one of them didn't shoot me on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you leave Rawa-Ruska?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or Lwow to go into, into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the estate of my grandfather is about, probably, 12 kilometers from Belzitz. Belzitz had a sugar beet refinery. And I used to go there with shipments of sugar beet, because it was interesting to observe it. Therefore, I knew the topography of the area, and I knew that Belzitz is a dead end. Every day, I could see 10 or 20 trains coming from the direction of Lwow, going to Belzitz, and coming back empty. There was no space, you see, for these people to be collected there and shipped, what they called, resettlement. So it was quite clear that something is happening to them. That's what's triggered, in my mind the attempt to escape and looking for ways, which was very difficult. Some people forged documents. Some were caught and shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a rather frightening experience to get out of the ghetto because this illusion that if you observe the law and the regulations, you might survive, this was something which was fed to these people all the time, by the Judenrats, by the [NON-ENGLISH], like [NON-ENGLISH], or Teicholz. I don't remember. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was here in New York. I, I read an obituary of his death two or three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I went to Lwow to consult with my mother. And then I told her that I would like to see the Metropolitan Sheptytsky, and at least safe guard the documents of my father, which she allowed me to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Metropolitan being that bishop?

speaker: interviewee
text: The archbishop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Archbishop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- how far was he from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably two miles, in the city though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They received me very warmly. And-- I described it in my book-- and asked me who I am. And he was very moved when he learned that I am the son of my father, because he was aware that he was killed. And he said, documents, documents. But I think I would like to save you and others. He was aware of what's going on. And he asked me to come back after three or four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he outline any kind of plan for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came back after three days, he told me that he would like to take my, my brother, who was, at that time, probably eight years old or nine years old, and place him with monks. And to discuss it with my mother and bring him to the palace. Well, my mother was not overly enthusiastic.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But eventually, she realized, because this was after the major actions, that, sooner or later, it will end badly. She was already on her way to Belzitz, so she agreed. So did my brother. So the next time, we took him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We being you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself, my mother, and my younger brother, who was nine years old. And we were received in the office of the Metropolitan of the archbishop. There was a priest waiting. He promised my mother that he would take care of him, be his guardian. And the priest took my brother, Father Kiprion. And that's the end of it. And I returned with my mother alone to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with Father Kiprion and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later on. But how long did you not have contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I went to visit my brother in the monastery where he lived. I went there. It's a place called [NON-ENGLISH], which is about, again, 70 miles from Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you, yourself--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came back. My mother told me, look, this boy is nine years old, and he cannot manage by himself. You have to go. And so I agreed to it. And I spoke to-- again, to the Metropolitan. It was fine. And I understood that if I get out, my mother will be eventually rescued. But she refused. And so I went away. That was sometime October 1942, about maybe a month and and a half before the ghetto was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go away? How did you disappear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very simple. [LAUGHS] I just walked to the palace with a little valise, a few of my belongings. And I came out of there without the Jewish arm band. And I was taken to an orphanage, where I was placed for a few days, and then taken to another monastery. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the monks knew that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know your brother was Jewish, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jewish children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, I found out, yes, quite a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like? What kind-- I mean, you weren't a little kid anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken into an orphanage. And how old were the orphans?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were teenagers, 12 to 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you expected to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. But this was a temporary place until-- you see, to place a Jew was not so easy-- [LAUGHS] --In those days. A reason being-- sorry to be vulgar-- but in Europe, Jews, only Jews are circumcised.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gentiles are not. And therefore, they didn't bother with any documents. Consequently, to place a Jew was not an easy business.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were only young boys that were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nuns took girls. I found girls eventually too. But that's much later. People didn't advertise it because for-- the penalty for saving a Jew or keeping a Jew was death. Therefore, the whole thing was done very discreetly. Now, who were your contacts in this orphanage? How long were you in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two or three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent me to next door. About maybe a kilometer from there was a man's monastery there, John the Baptist Monastery. There, I was kept for a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what were you-- were you using your own name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was already given this false name, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was kept locked up in this cell. My only contact was, was a priest, monk priest, who was teaching me language. I didn't speak Ukrainian, custom, culture, how to pray, and things of that sort. So I was, in a way, quickly trained to be adapted to my surrounding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with anybody other than that monk that was teaching you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I used to go to the refectory to have meals with the monks. I could wander around the monastery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, maybe not, because they thought that maybe I'm a novice. Then, slowly and gradually, it became clear to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep your own clothing, or did they give you monk's clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I had my own clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the food-- I ate the food of the monastery.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not an issue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the fact that their food may not have been kosher or anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. This, you see--

speaker: interviewer
text: For you? For you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: May I tell you something? I realize the morbid obsession with gastronomic rules, which the, nowadays, Jewish theologian had-- has. But under the circumstances--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --The question was not, you see, to have kosher food, but to have food to survive the day, first of all. And secondly, the monks did not worry about providing me with kosher food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an attempt to convert you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an attempt to have you be involved in you own religion as well, or to retain?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't, because, you see, I was observed all the time. The minute I would start-- [LAUGHS] --You see, any religious practices, I would endanger the whole community and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- did you think of-- what did you think of yourself? Who were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you think of yourself as Kurt Lewin? Or did you think of yourself as-- what was your false name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My false name was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you think of yourself as [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I knew that this is my name. And the same way, my brother knew that he had his name. It was different from mine in order not to make any connection in case either of us is caught. Were you in the same monastery?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Did you ever fear that you would be giving yourself away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in constant fear, constant fear. So were they. [LAUGHS] They were afraid that if I am caught, they will be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn to be Ukrainian? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] How does one learn to be a Ukrainian? Very simple. You see, as long as you realize that your life depends on it, you learn very quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about mannerisms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that I was taught, and I'm a very quick learner. So I had to learn, first of all, mannerisms. I have to-- I had to learn, very quickly, the language, and the ways of Ukrainian prayers, and behave in the church. I never went to the sacraments, but I had to make the right moves and the rhythm of what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you go to the sacraments?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I am not a Catholic. And I didn't want to-- respect-- they told me not to go to sacraments because of blasphemy, because they believe that the host is the body and-- of Christ.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of becoming a Catholic? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. It was not in my mind at that time because the question was the survival from day to day, from hour to hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in that-- in the monastery?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a variety of monasteries.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but the first one.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one, I would say probably close to 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you to get used to all of the-- the ways of living as a monk or a novice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably two months or three months. After three months, I was sufficiently trained that I could sing in the choir with them, learned old Slavonic, because they play in old Slavonic, and participate in all of the ceremonies without any problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you? Were they nice to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They treat me-- they treated me the same way as they treat me today. In their minds, I am one of them. And they treat me-- treated me as a friend, as part of the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 17, 16-- 17 years old?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- it's a different kind of feeling, I would imagine, when you are 17 and 18, then now. Did you suspect any kind of treatment that was different towards you than to-- towards other novices?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was treated the same way as I-- as everybody else. I worked with them, as everybody else, which was not easy. It was a very hard life, very difficult. We faced the danger the same way. And all I can say, curiously enough, I said in-- old conscience today, from perspective of 71 years of life and other experience, these were the happiest days of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? I lived with people who were decent, who tried to live by the gospels-- I didn't say that they were perfect-- who provided me with shelter and never made me feel that I'm an outsider, who accepted the same risk of being caught and shot, which faced me by the simple fact that I'm there. And while there was a storm all around me, I was, in a way, like in a Noah's arc. Were you aware that there were other Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slowly and gradually, because this was a silent order. And we communicated only half an hour a day in the circle of recreation. And then gradually, they let me know indirectly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they, you mean other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Monks.

speaker: interviewer
text: The monks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just hinted. One saw my brother somewhere, and he is well. Another mentioned that the Jew is sitting in the cellar. A third one told me that this German priest, Father Peters, keeps four Jewish families-- [LAUGHS] --In the cellar of the factory which he run, and so forth. And all of this was-- and one day I met a monk. He was Brother Daniel with a beard. And I asked him-- he didn't know that I'm Jewish because I already looked-- he was not in the same monastery as I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked him, Father Daniel, what are you doing? Well, he says, I'm, I'm running a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He had-- he ran an orphanage. And in this orphanage, there were three Ukrainian children and 17 Jewish children, boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he used the word [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he assume that you would know what it meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every Ukrainian knows what the [NON-ENGLISH] is. They lived together, you see, for centuries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any homosexuality in the monastery?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I've never encountered any homosexuality. None. It's only a figment of the imagination of the sick, Western mind, especially American mind. I lived in a monastery for two years in very close quarters. I'm sorry to disappoint you. I wasn't molested, and I haven't see anything, which I'm a pretty keen observer, as you can gather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm. Were there any narrow escapes where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Many. We're going to leave them for the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: What? For the next tape? Surely.
